coredata.dc:title: Ionic liquid-induced double regulation of carbon quantum dots modified bismuth oxychloride/bismuth oxybromide nanosheets with enhanced visible-light photocatalytic activity
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Qingsong
Ji, Mengxia
Di, Jun
Wang, Bin
Xia, Jiexiang
Zhao, Yaping
Li, Huaming
coredata.dc:description: The efficient separation of photoexcited electron-hole pairs acts as a significant factor and challenge for the enhanced photocatalytic activity of the photocatalyst. To pursue higher photocatalytic activity, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified bismuth oxychloride (BiOCl)/bismuth oxybromide (BiOBr) nanosheet photocatalyst has first been synthesized via an in situ ionic liquid-induced strategy. The bridge function of the ionic liquid ensures the uniform dispersal of CQDs on the surface of the BiOCl/BiOBr material. After the introduction of CQDs, the CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composite photocatalyst displayed enhanced photocatalytic activity for the photodegradation of several different types of organic contaminants such as rhodamine B, tetracycline hydrochloride, ciprofloxacin, and bisphenol A under the irradiation of visible light, and the BiOCl/BiOBr material loading with 5 wt% CQDs showed the best photocatalytic performance. The characterization results revealed that the introduction of CQDs could simultaneously improve the visible light absorption properties and separation efficiency of photoexcited electron-hole pairs. The electron spin resonance and radical quenching experiments demonstrated that during the photocatalytic reactions, holes and superoxide radicals were the main active species involved in the degradation of the contaminants, and the possible photocatalytic mechanism is presented. Therefore, this work provides an efficient pathway for the improved activity of the photocatalyst.
coredata.dcterms:subject: BiOCl/BiOBr nanosheets
Carbon quantum dots
Ionic liquids
Photocatalysis
Visible light irradiation

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of the as-prepared CQDs, BiOCl/BiOBr and CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composites with different loading amounts of CQDs.
XPS spectra of BiOCl/BiOBr and 5 wt% CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composite. (a) Survey of the samples; (b) Bi 4f; (c) Cl 2p; (d) Br 3d; (e) O 1s and (f) C 1s.
FT-IR spectra of CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composites with different loading amounts of CQDs.
Low magnification TEM images (a–c) and high magnification TEM image (d) of 5 wt% CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composite.

(a) Photocatalytic degradation of RhB in the presence of BiOCl, BiOBr, BiOCl/BiOBr, CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr and photolysis of RhB under visible light irradiation and (b) kinetic fits for the degradation of RhB with BiOCl, BiOBr, BiOCl/BiOBr, CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composites.
(a) Photocatalytic degradation of TC, (b) kinetic fits for the degradation of TC, photocatalytic degradation of (c) CIP and (d) BPA in the presence of BiOCl/BiOBr, CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composites.

(a) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of the as-prepared samples and CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composites with different loading amounts of CQDs (inset); (b) PL spectra of BiOCl/BiOBr and 5 wt% CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composite; (c) Transient photocurrent response for BiOCl/BiOBr and 5 wt% CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composite; (d) Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy of BiOCl/BiOBr and 5 wt% CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composite.

ESR spectra of the DMPO-O2
− adducts and DMPO-
OH recorded with (a, b) BiOCl/BiOBr and (c, d) 5 wt% CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composite under visible light irradiation.
Trapping experiments of active species during the photocatalytic degradation of RhB over 5 wt% CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composite under visible light irradiation.
Proposed charge transfer mechanism in the CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composites combined with the possible reaction mechanism of photocatalytic procedure.

head.ce:title: Ionic liquid-induced double regulation of carbon quantum dots modified bismuth oxychloride/bismuth oxybromide nanosheets with enhanced visible-light photocatalytic activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Ecological and Environmental Science, Shanghai Key Laboratory for Urban Ecological Processes and Eco-Restoration, East China Normal University, Shanghai 200241, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Ecological and Environmental Science
Shanghai Key Laboratory for Urban Ecological Processes and Eco-Restoration

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Institute for Energy Research, Jiangsu University, 301 Xuefu Road, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The efficient separation of photoexcited electron-hole pairs acts as a significant factor and challenge for the enhanced photocatalytic activity of the photocatalyst. To pursue higher photocatalytic activity, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modified bismuth oxychloride (BiOCl)/bismuth oxybromide (BiOBr) nanosheet photocatalyst has first been synthesized via an in situ ionic liquid-induced strategy. The bridge function of the ionic liquid ensures the uniform dispersal of CQDs on the surface of the BiOCl/BiOBr material. After the introduction of CQDs, the CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composite photocatalyst displayed enhanced photocatalytic activity for the photodegradation of several different types of organic contaminants such as rhodamine B, tetracycline hydrochloride, ciprofloxacin, and bisphenol A under the irradiation of visible light, and the BiOCl/BiOBr material loading with 5 wt% CQDs showed the best photocatalytic performance. The characterization results revealed that the introduction of CQDs could simultaneously improve the visible light absorption properties and separation efficiency of photoexcited electron-hole pairs. The electron spin resonance and radical quenching experiments demonstrated that during the photocatalytic reactions, holes and superoxide radicals were the main active species involved in the degradation of the contaminants, and the possible photocatalytic mechanism is presented. Therefore, this work provides an efficient pathway for the improved activity of the photocatalyst.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalysis, a very promising technique, can be applied in environmental remediation and the transformation of solar energy into chemical power [1,2]. A variety of photocatalysts such as (oxy)nitrides [3], oxides [4], plasmonic photocatalysts [5], and elemental photocatalysts [6] have been tested to attain the high-efficiency photocatalytic activity. Nevertheless, in practical applications, the performance of photocatalysts is greatly limited by two factors: (a) the narrow absorption range of visible light and (b) the inefficient separation and transport of photoexcited electron-hole pairs.

As one of the new layered materials, bismuth oxyhalides (BiOX, X = Cl, Br, I) have attracted growing research attention because of their excellent electrical, optical, and catalytic properties [7]. In all the BiOX photocatalysts, bismuth oxychloride (BiOCl) displays the highest photocatalytic activity under UV illumination [7,8]. Bismuth oxyiodide (BiOI) shows the best visible light absorption capacity because of its small energy gap. Bismuth oxybromide (BiOBr) reveals the highest photocatalytic oxidation and reduction activity under full light irradiation based on the appropriate band gap [7]. Nevertheless, the photocatalytic performance of individual bismuth oxyhalide is still limited to its low absorption of visible light, low transfer efficiency of photogenerated charges and high recombination rate of photoexcited electron-hole pairs. To improve the photocatalytic performance of BiOX, construction of BiOX-based composites with metallic salts (such as AgX [9], ZnFe2O4 [10], and NaBiO3 [11]), metal oxides (such as Fe3O4 [12], Bi2O3 [13], TiO2 [14], and WO3 [15]) and precious metals (such as Pt [16]) have been reported. In addition, BiOX/BiOY (X, Y = Cl, Br, I) have drawn increasing research attention for their light absorption ability, unique layered structure and effective separation of photogenerated carriers [17]. Li et al. [17] first developed a novel process for the solvent-thermal synthesis of BiOCl/BiOBr microspheres by employing ionic liquid as reaction solvents. An improved photocatalytic performance of BiOCl/BiOBr material has been obtained compared to BiOBr or BiOCl. Zhang [18] introduced microwave-assisted coprecipitation for the synthesis of heterostructured BiOCl/BiOBr photocatalyst. Comparing to BiOCl/BiOBr synthesized by the hydrothermal method, BiOCl/BiOBr synthesized by a microwave-assisted method showed a higher degradation rate of RhB. The high photocatalytic activity of BiOCl/BiOBr resulted mainly from the effective separation of photoexcited electron-hole pairs. However, it was difficult to regulate the size and crystal structure of BiOCl/BiOBr material using the microwave-assisted method. In addition, the narrow absorption of visible light and inefficient separation of photogenerated charge carriers for BiOCl/BiOBr material still remain, in spite of the improved photocatalytic activity of BiOBr or BiOCl.

To further enhance the photocatalytic activity of BiOX, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been introduced to extend the absorption of visible light and raise the separation efficiency of photoexcited electron-hole pairs [19]. Carbon quantum dots are a novel type of carbon-based material with one dimension less than 10 nm in size. They are composed of sp2/sp3 hybridized carbon atoms, have various surface functional groups and exhibit composition-dependent fluorescence [19]. CQDs have been widely used in a train of significant fields, for example, bio-imaging [20], catalysis [21], sensors [22], and photovoltaic devices [23], owing to the properties of easy functionalization, low toxicity, wonderful biocompatibility and fine resistance to photobleaching [24,25]. Meanwhile, due to the excellent properties of the electron transfer/reservoir, CQDs have been successfully doped into photocatalysts to improve the photocatalytic activity, for example, Fe2O3 [26], ZnO [27], Ag3PO4 [28], TiO2 [29], BiOX [30], Bi2WO6 [31], BiVO4 [32].

In this work, we report a one-step ionic liquid in situ induced solvent-thermal method for the preparation of two-dimensional CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composites with regulable loading amounts of CQDs for improving the degradation rate of rhodamine B (RhB), tetracycline hydrochloride (TC), ciprofloxacin (CIP) and bisphenol A (BPA) under visible light. The relationship between the photocatalyst structure and the photocatalytic activity is studied in detail. The photocatalytic reaction mechanism in this system is investigated by radical quenching experiments and electron spin resonance (ESR) analysis. This study could provide new sights into the design of more composite photocatalyst materials for the potential application to environmental remediation.

All the reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. The ionic liquid [C16mim]Cl (1-hexadecyl-3-methylimidazolium chloride) (99%) and [C16mim]Br (1-hexadecyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide) (99%) were purchased from Shanghai Chengjie Chemical Co., Ltd.

CQDs were prepared by a simple one-step process and then handled by freeze-drying [33]. Citric acid (1.05 g) was dissolved in 10 mL deionized water, and 335 μL of ethylenediamine was then added. The mixture was then poured into a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and reacted at 200 °C for 5 h, then cooled. The reddish-brown liquid was subjected to dialysis for 20 h to obtain the CQDs solution. Finally, the CQDs solid was obtained after freeze drying. The CQDs modified BiOCl/BiOBr composites were prepared via a facile solvent-thermal process. A given mass of CQDs and 0.48 g Bi(NO3)3·5H2O were dissolved in 9 mL, 0.1 M mannitol solution under magnetic stirring and defined as solution A. Ionic liquid [C16mim]Cl (0.17 g) and ionic liquid [C16mim]Br (0.19 g) were poured into 9 mL, 0.1 M mannitol solution to define solution B. B was added into A dropwise under continuous stirring and stirred for 0.5 h. The mixture was then poured into a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and reacted at 140 °C for 24 h. The composites that were obtained were centrifuged at high speed and washed with hot deionized water and ethanol five times, respectively. Finally, the composites were dried at 50 °C for 24 h. The added amount of CQDs in the CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composites was regulated to 1 wt%, 3 wt%, 5 wt% and 7 wt%, by regulating the mass ratio of CQDs to BiOCl/BiOBr. The BiOCl/BiOBr, BiOCl and BiOBr were also synthesized by a similar method without adding CQDs.

The phase composition of the photocatalysts was characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Shimadzu XRD-6000, Japan) employing Cu Kα as the source of radiation, λ = 1.54056 Å at scan speed of 7° min−1 over a range of 10–80°, operating at 40 mA and 40 kV. The morphology and nanosheet size of the materials were investigated by scanning electron microscope (SEM, JSM-7001F, JEOL, Japan), as well as by transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2010, JEOL, Japan). The chemical states of the photocatalysts were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) with a monochromatic Mg-Kα source operating at 20 kV. The UV–vis spectra of the prepared materials were obtained via a UV–vis spectrometer (UV-2450, Shimadzu, Japan) by the diffuse reflectance method with BaSO4 powder as the substrate. A certain amount of photocatalyst was mixed with the BaSO4 powder and then tabletted for measuring. The Tauc equation was then used to calculate the band gap of BiOCl, BiOBr and BiOCl/BiOBr. The specific surface area and gas adsorption isotherms of the materials at 77 K were measured by adsorption-desorption of N2 on a TristarⅡ 3020 surface area and porosity analyzer (Micromeritics Instrument Corporation, USA). FT-IR spectra were obtained on an FT-IR spectrophotometer (Nexus 470, Thermo Electron Corporation, USA) using a KBr disk as the reference with a spectral resolution of 4 cm−1 in the scanning range of 2000–400 cm−1. PL spectra of the materials were recorded employing a Varian Cary Eclipse spectrometer. The electron spin resonance (ESR) signals of spin-trapped radicals were recorded on an ESR spectrometer (JES-FA200, Bruker, Germany) by radical trapping reagent DMPO in methanol and water solution. The photocurrent and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were recorded with an electrochemical analyzer (CHI 660B).

Photocatalytic degradation of several different types of organic contaminants, such as rhodamine B (RhB), tetracycline hydrochloride (TC), bisphenol A (BPA) and ciprofloxacin (CIP) on CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composites was conducted to assess their photocatalytic activity. A 250 W Xe lamp with a 400 nm cutoff filter acted as the light source. Photocatalytic tests were performed in a circulating water system in case of thermal effects. In the photocatalytic experiments, 0.01 g, 0.03 g, 0.05 g and 0.05 g of photocatalysts were dispersed into 100 mL of RhB (10 mg L−1), 100 mL of TC (20 mg L−1), 100 mL of CIP (10 mg L−1) and 100 mL of BPA (10 mg L−1) solutions, respectively. Prior to irradiation, the contaminants and photocatalysts displayed adsorption-desorption equilibrium by being stirred continuously for 1 h. At predetermined time intervals, 4 mL suspensions were sampled and centrifuged at high speed to eliminate the photocatalyst nanoparticles. The concentrations of RhB, TC, and CIP were determined by employing the UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2450) on the basis of its absorbances at 553, 357 and 276 nm, respectively. BPA was analyzed with a Shimadzu LC-20A HPLC system (Shimadzu Corp, Kyoto, Japan) consisting of an Agilent TC-C (18) column, two Varian Prostar 210 pumps and a ultraviolet detector at 230 nm. A mobile phase consisting of acetonitrile and H2O in the ratio of 65:35 (v/v) was employed at the flow rate of 1 mL min−1. The pollutant solution (10 μL) was injected. To further investigate the generation of the main active species during the photocatalytic reactions, the radical quenching experiments were conducted by adding isopropyl alcohol (IPA), tert-butanol (tert-BuOH), nitrogen (N2) and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na).

To explore the photoelectrochemical properties, the modified electrodes of BiOCl/BiOBr and CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composites were prepared as follows: 5 mg of photocatalyst, and 1 mL of ethylene glycol were ultrasonically mixed to form a suspension and then 20 μL of the solution was dropped on the conductive side of the ITO glass (0.5 cm × 1 cm) and allowed to dry at 80 °C for 12 h. The electrochemical tests were conducted in a three-electrode system, with an ITO slice as the working electrode, a Pt wire as the counter electrode and a saturated Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode. Phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 0.1 M, pH 7.0) was employed as the supporting electrolyte during the PEC experiment. A 500 W Xe lamp acted as the light source. The EIS measurement was conducted in KCl solution (0.1 M) consisting of 5 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/Fe(CN)6 4−.

The structure and composition of BiOCl/BiOBr and CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composites with different loading amounts of CQDs were recorded via XRD, FT-IR, Raman and XPS analysis. The phase composition of CQDs, BiOCl/BiOBr, CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr, single BiOCl and BiOBr materials was determined by XRD (Fig. 1 and Fig. S1). All the diffraction peaks for the photocatalysts can be matched well with the tetragonal phase of BiOCl (JCPDS card No. 06-0249) and BiOBr (JCPDS card No. 09-0393). A broad peak at approximately 25° is observed in the XRD pattern of pure CQDs. No obvious peaks attributed to CQDs can be detected in the CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composites, which may be due to the small quantity and particle size of the CQDs dopant, as well as their high dispersion [30,31,33].

IR and Raman spectra were produced to further verify the existence of CQDs in the CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composites. Fig. 3 and Fig. S2 show the IR spectra of CQDs, BiOCl/BiOBr and CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composites with different weight ratios of CQDs. As depicted in Fig. S2, the peaks at 1439 cm−1 and 1570 cm−1 can be assigned to the COO– and CO stretching vibrations [38], revealing that there are COOH groups on the surface of the CQDs. After the formation of CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composites, the COO– and CO stretching vibrations of CQDs shift to 1460 cm−1 and 1598 cm−1, respectively [30] because of the successful interaction between CQDs and BiOCl/BiOBr [38]. The peak at 525 cm−1 can be owing to the BiO stretching vibration. The introduction of CQDs into the BiOCl/BiOBr material is also confirmed by Raman spectroscopy in Fig. S3. The G band of CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composites is relatively intense compared to the D band, which agrees with previous results because there is background interference in CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composites on the basis of the strong fluorescence of the CQDs [30].

The morphology of BiOCl/BiOBr and 5 wt% CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composite was studied by SEM and TEM. In Fig. S4, the as-prepared BiOCl/BiOBr material consists of nanosheets with sizes less than 100 nm. TEM analysis was conducted to further investigate the microstructure of CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composites (Fig. 4 ). Fig. 4a–c show typical low-magnification TEM images of the 5 wt% CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composite obtained. The near transparency of the sheets implies that the thickness of the nanosheets is thin. Fig. 4d displays the HRTEM image of the 5 wt% CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composite. The lattice fringes of the CQDs display an interplanar lattice spacing of 0.321 nm, which matches well with the (0 0 2) crystal plane [30]. The above results further demonstrate that CQDs have successfully distributed on the BiOCl/BiOBr nanosheets. Due to the elemental mapping images (Fig. S6), elements of C, Bi, Cl, Br and O are observed to distribute uniformly in the CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composites, which also proves that the CQDs have dispersed on the BiOCl/BiOBr surface to construct CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composites.

To investigate the effect of the porosity and surface area on the photocatalytic activity, the textural properties and N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of the prepared materials were investigated. As mentioned in Fig. S7, the N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms exhibit a type IV isotherm with a hysteresisloop, demonstrating the mesoporous characteristics of the obtained materials [33]. The specific surface area of BiOCl/BiOBr and the 5 wt% CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composite are calculated to be 35.60 m2 g−1 and 41.28 m2 g−1, respectively. The surface area has been improved by the introduction of CQDs. The improved specific surface area can enable the absorption of more contaminants and active species, and result in higher photocatalytic activity [39].

The photocatalytic performance of the as-prepared materials was first assessed by the photodegradation of RhB. Fig. 5 a displays the photocatalytic performance of CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composites with different loading amount of CQDs as well as BiOCl, BiOBr and BiOCl/BiOBr. The blank test without any photocatalyst was conducted to prove that the direct photolysis was nearly ignored. Compared to single BiOCl and BiOBr, BiOCl/BiOBr displays higher photocatalytic activity, which indicates that the BiOCl/BiOBr material efficiently inhibits the recombination of photogenerated carriers, and then more effective holes and electrons are involved in the photocatalytic reactions [17]. With the introduction of CQDs, the photocatalytic activity of BiOCl/BiOBr improves. The 5 wt% CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composite shows the best photocatalytic performance, compared with BiOCl/BiOBr and the hybrid material with other loading amounts of CQDs. When the CQDs content is higher than 5 wt%, the photocatalytic activity of BiOCl/BiOBr decreases. Although the modification of CQDs to BiOCl/BiOBr facilitates the charge transfer from BiOCl/BiOBr to CQDs, too many CQDs shadow the BiOCl/BiOBr surface from the incident visible light photons, leading to poor light exposure of BiOCl/BiOBr for the generation of photogenerated charge carriers [31,33]. Meanwhile, the photodegradation kinetics of RhB by the photocatalysts are investigated. As depicted in Fig. 5b, the concentration changes of RhB versus the irradiation time over the photocatalysts follow a pseudo-first-order kinetics plot. The 5 wt% CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composite displays the highest degradation rate, which is approximately 2.1, 2.8 and 3.0 times higher than that of BiOCl/BiOBr, BiOBr and BiOCl materials. The relative coefficients and pseudo-first-order constants are displayed in Table S1.

Tetracycline hydrochloride (TC), a common antibiotic agent, is widely employed for curing bacterial infections. The presence of TC in the environment poses severe threat to human health. Therefore, removal of TC is extremely important. Removal of TC is reported for the first time using CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composites for the TC photodegradation, and the data are shown in Fig. 6 a. In the presence of BiOCl/BiOBr, TC is photodegraded slowly under visible light. Approximately 23% TC is photodegraded after irradiation for 30 min. In contrast, the photocatalytic activity significantly improves after the introduction of CQDs, which indicates that CQDs play as a key role in the improvement of TC photodegradation. Of all the materials, the 5 wt% CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composite displays the best photodegradation performance. After irradiation for 30 min, 47% TC can be photodegraded. After the irradiation time extends to 120 min, the removal efficiency can reach 77%. Meanwhile, the photodegradation kinetics of TC over the BiOCl/BiOBr and CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composites are investigated, and the data are shown in Fig. 6b. The concentration changes of TC versus the irradiation time over the photocatalysts follow the pseudo-first-order kinetics plot. The 5 wt% CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composite displays the best degradation rate, which is approximately 2.5 times higher than the degradation rate of the BiOCl/BiOBr material. The relative coefficients and pseudo-first-order constants are summarized in Table S2.

Given the common organic contaminant photodegradation in actual application, the photocatalytic performance of CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composites was further assessed by the degradation of two other contaminants, CIP and BPA. In Fig. 6c and d, the CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composites display higher photocatalytic performance than BiOCl/BiOBr on CIP and BPA photodegradation. These degradation results of several different model contaminants imply that the introduction of CQDs is an efficient method to enhance photocatalytic activity, and the CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composites are high-efficiency visible-light responsive photocatalysts.

To investigate the optical absorption capability and energy gap of the materials obtained, the UV/vis diffuse reflectance spectrum was performed. In Fig. 7 a, the absorption edges of BiOBr, BiOCl/BiOBr and BiOCl, which are approximately 440 nm, 420 nm and 380 nm, respectively, decrease gradually. In contrast, pure CQDs display higher absorption of visible light. After the dispersion of CQDs on BiOCl/BiOBr nanosheets, the visible-light harvesting capability improves with the increase of the CQDs loading amounts. An obvious redshift of CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composites appears while comparing with BiOCl/BiOBr. The improvement of the visible light absorption may result in more photogenerated carriers [40], which in turn improves the photocatalytic activity.

Photoluminescence (PL) spectra, have been employed to demonstrate the separation, transfer and recombination processes of the photogenerated carriers in photocatalytic reactions. Stronger intensity implies a higher recombination rate of photogenerated carriers [31]. In Fig. 7b, both BiOCl/BiOBr and the 5 wt% CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composite show a strong emission peak located at 470 nm. For comparsion, when CQDs are dispersed on BiOCl/BiOBr nanosheets, the intensity of this emission band obviously weakens, which indicates that CQDs could efficiently suppress the recombination of photogenerated carriers [33].

Based on the experimental results mentioned, it is proposed that the introduction of CQDs could efficiently reduce the recombination efficiency of photogenerated carriers, which in turn improves the separation efficiency of photoexcited electron-hole pairs and enhances the photocatalytic activity. To further confirm this inference, photocurrents and EIS tests were conducted. Fig. 7c displays the photocurrent-time (I-t) plots of BiOCl/BiOBr and 5 wt% CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composite electrodes. The response of the photocurrent increases significantly once the light irradiation is activated. After six intermittent on-off irradiation cycles, photocurrents remain steady and reproducible. The 5 wt% CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composite shows higher photocurrent response than BiOCl/BiOBr, which implies that the CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composites display a higher separation rate of photoexcited electron-hole pairs. We speculate that the electrons are excited and migrate from the valence band to the conduction band of the photocatalyst and then transfer to CQDs, which in turn reduces the direct recombination of photoexcited electron-hole pairs. Owing to the conjugated π structure of CQDs, we conclude that CQDs are regarded as an excellent electron-acceptor material [41,42]. EIS results of BiOCl/BiOBr and the 5 wt% CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composite are shown in Fig. 7d. The EIS Nyquist plot of 5 wt% CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composite displays a much smaller arc radius than the EIS Nyquist plot of BiOCl/BiOBr, which implies that the fast transformation of interface charges to electron acceptor occurs and leads to efficient separation of electron-hole pairs [43]. The results of EIS and photocurrents coincide exactly with the PL measurements, which confirm that the introduction of CQDs is an effective approach to enhance photocatalytic activity.

The stability and reusability of the photocatalyst are of utmost importance in addition to the photocatalytic performance. Recycling experiments were conducted via the photodegradation of RhB, TC, CIP and BPA by 5 wt% CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composite. In Fig. S8, after five sequential cycles, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency remains nearly constant, which indicates the high stability of CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composites. Moreover, the structure of the 5 wt% CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composite before and after the photocatalytic reactions was measured by XRD. Fig. S9 shows that no significant changes of the crystal structure are obtained after five photocatalytic cycles, which clearly confirms that the as-prepared samples remain stable during the photodegradation process.

Most photodegradation processes of organic contaminants involve the utilization of the photogenerated holes with sufficient oxidative capacity in the valence band, or the exploitation of other photogenerated active species such as hydroxyl radical (
OH) and superoxide radical (O2
−), which originate from the holes with the surface adsorbed hydroxyl group or water and the reaction of electrons with the surface adsorbed oxygen, respectively [44]. The ESR technique was conducted to explore the main active species generated by BiOCl/BiOBr and CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composites during the photocatalytic reactions under visible light [45]. In Fig. 8 a, the characteristic peaks of DMPO-O2
− are observed for BiOCl/BiOBr, indicating the generation of O2
− over BiOCl/BiOBr under visible light. Because the DMPO-hydroxyl radical (
OH) species is not detected, the
OH is not involved in the photocatalytic reactions (Fig. 8b). For the CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composites, the intensity of DMPO-O2
− signals is enhanced greatly after the dispersion of CQDs on BiOCl/BiOBr nanosheets (Fig. 8c). Because the O2
− is generated from O2 reduction through one-electron transfer, the higher O2
− intensity of the CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composites reveals that CQDs enable more photogenerated electrons to reduce O2, attributed to its special delocalized conjugated structure [33], which renders it able to transfer the photogenerated electrons easily, resulting in effective separation of photoexcited electron-hole pairs. A similar phenomenon is observed in Fig. 8d, which indicates that the
OH is not involved in the photocatalytic reactions for CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr.

To gain a better understanding of the main active species in the photocatalytic reactions over CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composites, a variety of radical quenching experiments were conducted by adding different scavengers (IPA and t-BuOH for
OH, N2 for O2
−, EDTA-2Na for holes) [33]. As shown in Fig. 9 , when IPA or t-butanol is added, the photodegradation efficiency is not obvious affected, coinciding with the ESR data. The
OH is not the main reactive species for CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composites. However, with the addition of N2 or EDTA-2Na, the photodegradation performance is significantly inhibited, which confirms that holes and O2
− are the main active species involved in the photocatalytic reactions.

It is commonly established that high photocatalytic activity is owing to the intense absorption of visible light and high separation efficiency of photoexcited electron-hole pairs [15,46]. Moreover, the positions of the band edge for BiOCl and BiOBr are estimated as follows [18]:
(1)
E VB
= X -
E
e
+ 0.5
E g

where E VB and E CB are the valence band (VB) edge potential and conduction band (CB) edge potential, and X is the geometric mean of the electronegativity of the constituent atoms, which represents the electronegativity of the photocatalyst. E e represents the energy of free electrons on the hydrogen scale (∼4.5 eV), and E g is the energy gap of the photocatalyst. The X values for BiOBr and BiOCl are calculated to be 6.17 and 6.33 [47]. From DRS analysis (Fig. S10), the E g values of BiOBr and BiOCl are determined to be 2.90 and 3.31 eV. The E VB results of BiOBr and BiOCl are 3.12 and 3.48 eV, and thus, the E CB results of BiOBr and BiOCl are 0.22 and 0.17 eV, respectively. In this system, the holes and O2
− are determined to be the main active species involved in photocatalytic reactions based on ESR analysis (Fig. 8) and radical quenching experiments (Fig. 9). The
OH is measured not to be the main reactive species by the quenching experiments. No characteristic signals for the DMPO-hydroxyl radical (
OH) are observed from ESR analysis. In contrast, the VB values of BiOCl (3.48 eV) and BiOBr (3.12 eV) are higher than E0(
OH/OH–) (2.38 eV vs NHE) [33]. As a result, the OH– could be oxidized to generate
OH by the hVB +. Meanwhile, the CB values of BiOCl (0.17 eV) and BiOBr (0.22 eV) cannot reduce O2 to generate O2
− because the (O2/O2
−) is nearly -0.046 eV (vs NHE) [48]. However, this result is inconsistent with the radical quenching experiments and ESR analysis in this system. To gain further insight into the incompatible result mentioned above, the XPS valence spectra are adopted. The information about the total density of states (DOS) of extra electronic states above the valence band of a semiconductor can be obtained based on the XPS valence spectra [49]. The valence band (VB) of BiOCl and BiOBr was measured to be 1.35 and 2.01 eV, respectively [30,33]. Thus, the E CB was estimated to be −1.96 and −0.89 eV. As a result, the generation of O2
− can be obtained, while
OH cannot be observed.

Because of the above experimental results in this work and related references [17,18,30,33], a mechanism can be proposed. For the CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composites (Fig. 10 ), both BiOCl and BiOBr could be motivated to generate electron-hole pairs under visible light. The excited electrons in the CB of BiOCl (−1.96 eV) will quickly transfer to the CB of BiOBr (−0.89 eV). In contrast, the photogenerated holes of BiOBr (2.01 eV) will accumulate in the VB of BiOCl (1.35 eV). This process can reduce the recombination of photoexcited electron-hole pairs as formed. When CQDs are introduced, these excited electrons on the CB of BiOCl/BiOBr transfer to CQDs. The special delocalized conjugated structure makes CQDs transfer the excited electrons easily, which results in high efficiency separation of electron-hole pairs and the enhancement of photocatalytic performance in agreement with the results of PL, EIS and photocurrent analysis. At the same time, the electrons on the CQDs can reduce the adsorbed oxygen molecule to form O2
−. The generated O2
− and holes are the main active species involved in the photocatalytic reactions, which leads to dramatic photocatalytic activity.

On the basis of the previous study [17,30,39], BiOCl/BiOBr nanosheets have been prepared via a facile hydrothermal method. During the process, ionic liquid act as both reactant and high-performance template to facilitate the structural regulation of functional materials. After the introduction of CQDs, ionic liquid could further act as the dispersing agent, which is benefical for the uniform dispersal of CQDs on the surface of BiOCl/BiOBr nanosheets. The 5 wt% CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composite displayed the optimal visible light-driven photocatalytic activity for the degradation of RhB, TC, CIP and BPA. Furthermore, the 5 wt% CQDs/BiOCl/BiOBr composite displayed excellent stability and reusability, which suggested its promising applications in environmental remediation. The significant enhancement of photocatalytic activity was due to the enhanced visible light absorption and high separation efficiency of photoexcited electron-hole pairs, which was due to the introduction of CQDs. The holes and O2
− were the main active species involved in the photocatalytic reactions. This work can provide a novel way to design easy, high efficiency and stable photocatalysts for the elimination of organic contaminants in a natural environment.
This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21476098, 21471069 and 21576123), and Jiangsu University Scientific Research Fund (No. 11JDG0146).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2018.02.057.
